pages.text: Effect of elevated temperature on physico-mechanical properties of blended cement concrete Hosam.El_Din H. Seleem a, Alaa M. Rashad a,⇑, Tarek. Elsokary b a Building Materials Research and Quality Control Institute, Housing & Building National Research Center, HBRC, Cairo, Egypt b Raw Building Materials Technology and Processing Research Institute, Housing & Building National Research Center, HBRC, Cairo, Egypt article info Article history: Received 8 December 2009 Received in revised form 11 May 2010 Accepted 19 June 2010 Available online 30 August 2010 Keywords: Heat resistive Residual strength Elevated temperature Pozzolana abstract Pozzolanas are readily available for use in concrete in the local markets for strength and/or durability enhancement. Although safety and security against disasters are not new, they still presuming a chal- lenge. For instance, the ﬁre resistive properties of concrete are of prime interest. Through this work, the effect of different kinds of pozzolana on the ﬁre resistive properties of concrete was studied. Four types of pozzolana were incorporated into the concrete mixtures, i.e. metakaolin (MK), silica fume (SF), ﬂy ash (FA), and ground granulated blast furnace slag (GGBS). Each of the employed poz- zolana was used in two ratios: 10% and 20%, either in the form of cement replacement or as an addition without affecting the cement content. A total of 17 mixes were cast. For all mixtures, compressive strength is evaluated after 28 days of water curing. The mixtures’ com- pressive strengths were also evaluated after exposure to elevated temperatures: 200 /C176C, 400 /C176C, 600 /C176C, and 800 /C176C. The residual compressive strengths after heat exposure are evaluated. The formed cementi- tious phases after incorporation of pozzolana and the heat-induced transformations are investigated via the X-ray diffraction technique (XRD). Test results demonstrate the impact of each type of the employed pozzolana on the heat resistive prop- erties of concrete in addition to their inﬂuence on the strength development of the investigated mixes. Therefore, a decision could be made regarding optimizing the beneﬁts speciﬁc to each type of pozzolana and their employment method. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Although concrete is generally believed to be an excellent ﬁre- prooﬁng material, many recent studies have shown extensive dam- age or even catastrophic failure at high temperatures, particularly in high strength concrete [1]. Pozzolanic materials are customarily employed as an active addition or substitution to the ordinary Portland cement in concrete mixtures due to their capacity for reacting with lime, and the resulting enhancement in strength and durability of concrete. In a recent investigation, it was found [2] that metakaolin improves the ﬁre resistive properties of mortar till a replacement ratio of 20%. In another work [3], paste mixes made with 30% ﬂy ash exhibited residual strength double that of the control mix. Diederichs et al. [4] prepared three high strength concrete mixes incorporating silica fume, ﬂy ash, and granulated blast furnace slag independently. The mixes were subjected to a maximum temperature of about 900 /C176C. The slag concrete showed the best performance followed by ﬂy ash mix and then the silica fume concrete. It was postulated [5,6] that with increasing temperature, there would be a decrease of compressive strength, density, thermal con- ductivity, and th

concrete leading to dehydration and shrinkage of the cement matrix. The thermo-hygral interaction determines the multi- phase transport of water in the liquid, moisture in the vapor, and air in the gaseous phases. 2. Experimental program The experimental program was designed to explore the inﬂuence of four differ- ent types of pozzolana on the ﬁre resistive properties of concrete. The employed pozzolana are silica fume (SF), type F ﬂy ash (FA), ground granulated blast furnace slag (GGBS), and metakaolin (MK). These types are the most available in the local market, beside the wide variations in their physico-chemical properties. Four series of mixes in addition to the control mix were cast. Each series corre- sponds to a speciﬁc type of pozzolana which was employed either as a partial replacement of cement in two ﬁxed ratios, i.e. 10%, and 20% or as an addition in the same two ratios. A total of 17 mixes were cast and their residual (retained) strengths after heat exposure are evaluated. Visual observations regarding their spalling characteristics were also recorded. A comparison of the formed cementi- tious phases before and after exposure to 800 /C176C temperature of all mixes was also made. 2.1. Materials The employed aggregates are natural siliceous sand has a ﬁneness modulus of 2.64, and crushed dolomite stone with more than 90% of its particles have a size of 20 mm. Ordinary Portland cement (CEM I) of Blain surface area 3350 cm 2/g and comply with the relevant Egyptian standard speciﬁcations is used through this work. The four pozzolanic materials are silica fume containing about 95% SiO 2, type F ﬂy ash, metakaolin, and ground granulated blast furnace slag. The ﬁrst two types of pozzolana were used as received from the manufacturer, but the other two types were specially processed before use. kaolin was heated at about 850 /C176C for 2 h then brought out the furnace at Lab temperature to cool down rather fast, thereby en- ables the formation of amorphous glassy phases with pozzolanic nature, i.e. metakaolin. The granulated blast furnace slag was delivered from El-Tebin factory of steel in the form of water quenched ﬁne grains. The slag was then ﬁnely ground in a laboratory ball mill, the fraction passing 100 lm sieve is used through this work. Table 1 outlines the chemical compositions of the employed pozzolanas. 2.2. Mixes features In the following, a summary of the main features of the designed concrete mix- tures shown in Table 2 : /C15The water content is maintained the same in all mixtures, as it was planned to accomplish an appropriate level of ﬂowability via HRWR. /C15The employed high-range water reducer (HRWR) has a sulphonated naphtha- lene base is used at a dosage of 2.0% from the cementitious materials weight to accomplish high ﬂowability. /C15The coarse aggregate to sand ratio is 1.8 by weight. /C15The pozzolanic materials were employed either as a partial replacement of cement in two ﬁxed ratios, i.e. 10%, and 20% or as an addition in the same two ratios. 3. Specimens and methods of investigation Mixing operations of the concrete mixtures were sequenced as follows: all the ﬁne materials, i.e. cement and pozzolana were care- fully mixed in a pan mixer. The HRWR is premixed with the water and about two thirds of it was added to the mixer and mixing oper- ations continued until the mixture became homogenous after about 120 s. Sand was implemented to the wet mixture and mixing further continued to assure complete homogen

exposed to the predetermined temperatures with 200 /C176C interval. After 3 h of exposure to the elevated temperature, the electrical furnace was turned off and the specimens were allowed to cool down for about 2 h at the furnace environment. The specimens were then brought out the furnace and left in the room environ- ment to cool down. Subsequently after that, the specimens were tested in compression. The loading of the specimens follows the deformation control technique with a constant deformation rate of about 0.25 mm/min. The residual (retained) strengths after ele- vated temperature exposure were evaluated in relation to the 28 day strength. A mineralogical study was conducted employing the X-ray dif- fraction technique (XRD) to identify the formed phases before and after exposure to 800 /C176C. After performing the compressive strength test, the crushed concrete cubes of each mix were ﬁnely ground and thoroughly mixed. A representative sample from each mix was undertaken and ground to a very ﬁne powder that passes (75 lm) sieve and was tested immediately after that. 4. Results and discussion 4.1. Compressive strength The effect of the different kinds of pozzolana on the concrete compressive strength at ages of 7 days and 28 days is shown in Figs. 1 and 2 respectively. Different contribution of each type of pozzolana could be observed which is dependent on the employ- ment method whether replacement or addition. Before interpret- ing theses results, the following statements worth to be mentioned: /C15The water content is the same in all mixes. /C15The content of HRWR is dependent on the cementitious materi- als content, but its contribution to the total water content is generally small. /C15The mixes containing pozzolana exhibited higher ﬂowability than the control one, especially those containing ﬂy ash and slag and these results will be presented elsewhere. /C15The speciﬁc weights of the different pozzolanas are not equal which result in a different paste volumes and consequently aggregate volume for the same employment method (addition or replacement) and ratio. The inﬂuence of each kind of the employed pozzolana on con- crete compressive strength, shown in Figs. 1 and 2 , could be sum- marized as follows: /C15The silica fume enhanced the strength signiﬁcantly at all ages especially when used as an addition. The increase in the 7 day strength over that of the control mix was up to 19%, and 45% for the cases of replacement and addition respectively. The increase was up to 50% and 75% respectively for the 28 day strength. /C15The ﬂy ash contribution to strength seems to be more efﬁcient at the early ages. The increase in the 7 day strength over that of Fig. 1. 7-Day compressive strength of the tested mixes. Fig. 2. 28-Day compressive strength of the tested mixes. H.E.H. Seleem et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1009–1017 1011

part of their strengths after elevated temperature exposure is also explained by the formation of refractory compounds from the min- erals that may present in the pozzolanic materials composition. Exposing the concrete specimens to a higher level of tempera- ture, 800 /C176C, generally causes more physico-chemical transforma- tions to take place. For instance, re-crystallization of new compounds, and also more relative expansions (or shrinkage) be- tween the concrete constituents. Figs. 3–6 show that the highest strength value amongst all results is 16 MPa assigned for the case of 20% silica fume addition, which is about 33% of the correspond- ing 28-day strength (strength retention ratio). The second one in order is 15.53 MPa for the case of 10% metakaolin addition corre- sponding to 41.2% strength retention ratio. The third one in order is 14.98 MPa assigned for the case of 20% ﬂy ash addition and cor- responding to the highest recorded strength retention ratio, which is 43.3%. As could be realized, the metakaolin appears to be the most appropriate for heat resistance purpose because it possessed the same efﬁciency as the other two types of pozzolana at half of their employment doses. Visual observations of the test specimens after exposure to the different testing temperatures reveal that no spalling or disintegra- tion was noticed in any of the tested specimens upon heating that could be due to adopting a relatively high (water/cement) ratio which would generate more connected pores. 4.3. Interpretation of XRD results The X-ray diffractograms of the metakaolin (MK) group of mixes at ambient temperature and at 800 /C176C are shown in Fig. 7 a and b respectively. Before heating (at ambient temperature) perhaps the most important diffraction peaks to be traced are the ones cor- responding to calcium hydroxide (CH). As could be seen, the efﬁ- ciency of MK as a pozzolana is well recognized as the CH diffraction peaks is reduced with increasing the quantity of MK either as a replacement or as an addition. The reduction in the free CH quantity is acknowledged for strength and durability issues. Unfortunately, the two employment methods of MK can not easily be distinguished from each other as a result of the relatively high peaks of quartz mineral (from sand). As a consequent, the other peaks of the other compounds appeared relatively small. Fig. 7 a also shows that, the mineral calcium aluminates (CA) is present in the MK concrete mixes, while is not present in the control mix. This result is expected because the MK pozzolana contains a relatively large quantity of (Al 2O3), about 35.5% as shown in Table 1. After heating to 800 /C176C, as discussed before, the MK mixes re- tained a relatively high ratio of their 28 day strength over that of the control mix. This superior performance can be explained partly due to the reduction in the CH quantity as a main source of insta- bility. Also, re-crystallization of new compounds from existing materials shares in maintaining a reasonable level of strength after heating well above that of the control mix. Fig. 7 b shows that, the CH is no far present, and the found calcite is probably from the decomposition of the dolomite aggregate and recarbonation of CH. More importantly, the production of hydrothermally new phases is recognized in the MK mixes. The newly formed phases are: calcium aluminates (CAH 10), calcium silicates (C 2SH, and C3S2H3), and calcium aluminosilicates (CAS 2H4). These phases are

the control mix was up to 11%, and 41% for the cases of replace- ment and addition respectively. At 28 days age, these ratios decrease to 9%, and 25% respectively. /C15The slag efﬁciently enhanced the strength when employed as an addition by a ratio up to 40% at both ages 7, and 28 days. That was not the case for the replacement method, as the enhance- ment in strength was only marginal. /C15The efﬁciency of metakaolin as a pozzolana is apparent only when used as an addition. In this case, the strength increase over that of the control mix was up to 26%, and 45% at the ages of 7 days, and 28 days respectively. For the case of replacement, there was a reduction in the strength at 7 days and a marginal increase at 28 days age. Anyhow, the strength of the mix con- taining 20% addition of metakaolin is superior to the other mixes containing ﬂy ash, or slag which appreciate metakaolin as an alternative to silica fume if proper dosage and employ- ment method are adopted. 4.2. The response of concrete mixes to thermal loading Exposing the cementitious products to elevated temperatures is normally associated with multiple chemico-physical transforma- tions, which would affect the stability of the internal structure and consequently the strength. The most recognized causes of these transformations include; dehydration (decomposition) of the cementitious compounds, different expansion values of the constituents (thermal mismatch), and internal pore pressure. The behavior of the investigated concrete mixes incorporating four different kinds of pozzolana under different thermal loads is evaluated quantitatively by measuring the retained (residual) strengths after heat exposure and qualitatively by visual inspec- tions. Four different temperatures were chosen for thermal load- ing, i.e. 200 /C176C, 400 /C176C, 600 /C176C, and 800 /C176C. The results are shown in Figs. 3–6 . It was generally noted that the compressive strength of all mixes increases upon heating to 200 /C176C over the 28 day strength of the corresponding mixes. That is partly attributed to the evap- oration of free water which leads to friction increase between failure planes, or possibly this level of heat catalyze hydration of the non-reacted cementitious products, i.e. advancement in chemical bounding process. Anyhow, the most pronounced in- crease is for silica fume-mixes. That could be attributed to its ul- tra ﬁneness which promotes the pozzolanic reaction of the silica grains. Fig. 3. Heat strength proﬁle: case of 10% replacement with pozzolana. Fig. 4. Heat strength proﬁle: case of 20% replacement with pozzolana. 1012 H.E.H. Seleem et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1009–1017

It was also noted that at 400 /C176C, the compressive strengths of all concrete mixes are slightly affected by the temperature increase. A slight reduction in the compressive strength as compared to the 28 day strength was noticed for the control mix as well as the mixes with 10% pozzolana addition. The other mixes correspond- ing to the other cases of pozzolana employment possessed slight increase in their strengths over that of the 28 day ones. Anyhow, it is rather difﬁcult at this level of temperature to distinguish be- tween the efﬁciencies of different pozzolanas in providing the con- crete mixes with a satisfactory level of stability at elevated temperature. To facilitate tracing the effect of pozzolana on the ﬁre resistive properties of concrete, the test results are presented in two differ- ent forms either as the retained (residual) compressive strength values after heat exposure, or the ratio of these strengths to the 28 day ones (before heat exposure). At 600 /C176C, the possible chemical transformations include; decomposition of the cementing compound CSH with its different phases, dehydration of calcium hydroxide (CH) into free lime, a–b quartz transformation. These changes would affect the volume occupied by these cementitious products and when combined with the weakened cohesion between the mixture constituents due to the different expansions experienced by each of them that might develop micro cracks or hairs inside the concrete mass and conse- quently degradation of the compressive strength would result. The ﬁgures show that at 600 /C176C, the retained strength of the control mixture is about 64% of the 28-day compressive strength. On the other hand, the mixtures containing pozzolana were able to retain a considerable part of their 28 day strengths up to 84%, 79%, 81%, and 94% for the cases of 10% replacement with ﬂy ash, 20% replacement with slag, 10% silica fume addition, and 20% ﬂy ash addition respectively. Anyhow, the retained compressive strength values of the silica fume-mixes were the highest for all of the employment methods. Apparently the pozzolana is able via its pozzolanic nature to combine with some of the free CH to form the cementing compound CSH, and thus reducing the amount of CH as a main source of instability upon heating. It was explained [7] that decomposition of CH into CaO and its subsequent reaction with the humidity from the surrounding air during cooling seems to cause micro cracks due to the volume increase associated with this process. The pozzolanic activity which reduces the CH partly explains the superior resistance of the mixtures incorporating poz- zolanic materials to degradation at elevated temperatures. The superior ability of the pozzolana-mixes to retain a considerable Fig. 5. Heat strength proﬁle: case of 10% pozzolana addition. Fig. 6. Heat strength proﬁle: case of 20% pozzolana addition. H.E.H. Seleem et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1009–1017 1013

Afwillite (C 3S2H3). The ﬁgure shows that the peak intensities of these two compounds are more intensive at a ratio 10% of silica fume for both of the two employment methods. This result is not expected, as increasing the content of silica fume are supposed to be more prone to the refractory compounds. It is interesting to note that at 20% silica fume, whether replacement or addition, the retained strength ratio of concrete is much less than it for the case of 10% silica fume replacement. Nevertheless, the highest recorded residual strength value amongst all mixes is exhibited by the mix containing 20% silica fume addition. Thus, it is rather dif- ﬁcult to decide which one is more heat resistant, and which mea- sure is more appropriate for this purpose: the retained strength ratio or the residual strength value. Anyhow, more research work is still needed to establish approved measuring criteria. Fig. 9 a and b show the X-ray diffractograms of the ﬂy ash (FA) concrete mixes before and after exposure to 800 /C176C, respectively. Fly ash like other kinds of pozzolanas is efﬁcient in reducing the amount of free CH as could be realized by comparing the calcium hydroxide main peak identiﬁed at d spacing 2.628 Å (2 h = 34.1/C176) and the other conﬁrming peaks of the control mix and the ﬂy ash mixes, Fig. 9 a. The FA pozzolana owing to its relatively high con- tent of alkalis promotes formation of Stratlingite compound although the corresponding peaks are relatively small. The forma- tion of this compound is important because it is thermally stable and therefore contributes to the thermal stability of the concrete mixtures. After heating to 800 /C176C, many hydrothermally formed minerals could be seen in the FA mixes while they are not recog- nizable in the control mix. The formed compounds are Afwillite (C3S2H3), CAH10, and Stratlingite (C 2ASH8). However, the diffrac- tion peaks of these compounds in some of the samples were broad or poorly developed and in some cases overlap. It was observed that, the peak intensities of these compounds slightly increase and their crystallinity improves with increasing the ﬂy ash content and is more apparent in the case of addition employment method more than for the replacement method. The oxide analysis shown in Table 1 shows that ﬂy ash contains a relatively higher amount of alkalis (Na2O and K 2O) than the other pozzolanic materials. The al- kali ions are thought [8] to increase the dissolution of silicates and therefore promote formation of the cementitious compounds. The relatively high content of alkalis explains the formation of the refractory compounds Pectolite (NC 4S6H), and Plagioclase (0.7NAS2/C10.3CAS2) in the FA concrete mixes. Although the peak intensities of these compounds are relatively small but when gath- ered with the other formed refractory peaks constitute a rather heat enduring material. These results are in a good agreement with the retained compressive strength after exposure to 800 /C176C, as the mix contains 20% FA addition scored the highest retained strength ratio. Fig. 10 a shows the X-ray diffractograms of the control mix as well as the ground granulated slag (GGBS) containing mixes before heat exposure. As could be seen, the calcium hydroxide (CH) is somewhat reduced in the slag-mixes. The reductions are less pro- nounced than for the other kinds of employed pozzolanic materi- als. That could be due to the less efﬁciency of slag as a pozzolana than the other employed pozzola

Anyhow, the formation of several types of the heat resistive compounds in the 800 /C176C exposed slag-mixes, shown in Fig. 10 b, prevented further drop in the retained strength ratio. The ﬁgure shows that the same compounds formed in the MK mixes are formed in the slag-mixes although they are of lesser peaks. An- other compounds are formed due to the relatively high magnesium oxide content, i.e. Merwinite (C 3MS2), Akermanite (C2MS2), and the Spinel magnesium aluminate (MgAl2O4). It is generally agreed that, these compounds have a good refractoriness and resistance to thermal shock. 5. Conclusions Through this work, it was found that employing pozzolanic materials within the concrete constituents appreciably enhances its ﬁre resistive properties. This is attributed partly to the pozzola- nic reactions, which consume a considerable part of CH, and partly to the formation of refractory compounds from the minerals of the pozzolanas composition. The following conclusions are drawn based on the test results: /C15The efﬁciency of all types of the employed pozzolanas regarding strength enhancement is more apparent when employed as an addition. The increase in the concrete 28 day strength over that of the control mix were up to 75%, 45%, 27%, and 40% for the case of addition of silica fume, metakaolin, ﬂy ash, and ground granulated slag (GGBS) respectively. The corresponding increases for the case of replacement with the same pozzolanas were up to 50%, 3%, 9%, and 11% respectively. /C15Upon heating to 200 /C176C, it was generally noted that the com- pressive strength of all mixes increases over the 28-day strength of the corresponding mixes. This is partly attributed to the evaporation of free water, which leads to friction increase between failure planes. In addition, this level of heat enhances self-autoclaving, which accelerates the rate of hydration of cement phases and pozzolanic reaction. /C15At 400 /C176C, the compressive strengths of all pozzolana–concrete mixes are slightly affected by the temperature increase. A slight reduction in the compressive strength as compared to the 28- day strength was noticed for the control mix as well as the mixes with 10% pozzolana addition. The other mixes corre- sponding to the other cases of pozzolana employment pos- sessed slight increase in their strengths over that of the corresponding 28-day ones. /C15At 600 /C176C, the control mix retained about 64% of its 28-day com- pressive strength. On the other hand, the mixes containing poz- zolana were able to retain a considerable part of their 28-day strengths up to 80%, 82%, 94%, and 80% corresponding to the employment of silica fume, metakaolin, ﬂy ash, and GGBS respectively. Anyhow, the retained (residual) compressive strength values of the silica fume-mixes are the highest. /C15Heating to 800 /C176C caused a signiﬁcant reduction in strength of all mixes. The retained strength ratio of the control mix dropped to 32%. The pozzolana-mixes possessed a better strength reten- tion ratios; up to 41%, 41%, 43%, and 36% corresponding to the employment of silica fume, metakaolin, ﬂy ash, and GGBS respectively. The highest residual strength value amongst all results is 16 MPa assigned for the case of 20% silica fume addi- tion, which is about 33% of the corresponding 28-day strength (strength retention ratio). /C15X-ray diffraction analysis revealed the formation (re-crystalliza- tion) of hydrothermally new compounds in the pozzolana- mixes. For instance: – Cal
